name: Highway Border
description: Strip along highway frontage. Primarily mowed turf with scattered volunteer trees.
goals: Establish native wildflower and grass buffer. Reduce mowing frequency over time.
notes: Check local mowing ordinances before reducing mow frequency. Highway ROW may be county-maintained — confirm property boundaries.

name: Berm Woodland
priority_action: URGENT: Remove all white mulberry. Cut and immediately treat stumps with concentrated glyphosate. Repeat every 6 weeks through growing season.
description: Raised berm with mixed woodland. Significant white mulberry invasion threatening native understory.
goals: Native woodland restoration. Favor bur oak, hackberry, and native understory shrubs. Eliminate mulberry and garlic mustard.
notes: Mulberry spreads aggressively via bird droppings — remove all fruiting trees first. Scout for garlic mustard in spring and treat before seed set. It spreads by the thousands once established.

name: Plateau / Fire Pit
description: Higher plateau area with fire pit. Mixed use area with some native plantings.
goals: Maintain as relaxed native prairie/savanna mix. Low maintenance once established.
notes: Keep fire pit area clear 10-foot radius. Native grasses thrive here; prescribed burns would benefit this zone long-term. Good location for fall seeding.

name: Home / Drive / Pool
description: Maintained home grounds including driveway, pool area, and ornamental plantings. Managed turf lawn.
goals: Maintain existing ornamentals. Monitor blue spruces for disease. Gradually replace turf with native groundcovers where practical.
notes: Blue spruce trees present — monitor for Rhizosphaera needle cast. Treat with Daconil when new growth reaches 1–2 inches in spring. Second application may be needed if pressure is high.

name: Creekside
priority_action: #1 PRIORITY: Cut ALL bush honeysuckle and paint stumps with concentrated glyphosate every 6 weeks through the growing season. It regrows vigorously — do not skip cycles.
description: Creek corridor running west to east. Wet-tolerant species zone. Young bur oak present in NW corner — protect it. Bush honeysuckle is the #1 threat.
goals: Eliminate bush honeysuckle entirely. Establish wet-tolerant native riparian corridor. Protect and nurture the young bur oak in NW corner.
notes: Young bur oak in NW corner — FLAG IT and protect from deer browse. Creek slope runs west to east. Honeysuckle leafs out 2–3 weeks before native plants — easiest to spot and treat in early spring before natives green up.

name: Island
description: Small isolated planting area. Contains established lilacs. Low maintenance zone.
goals: Maintain lilacs. Add shade-tolerant native companions that won't compete aggressively.
notes: Prune lilacs in spring immediately after bloom — OK through early summer. NEVER prune in September or fall — this removes next year's flower buds. Lilacs bloom on old wood.

name: NE Seed Plot
priority_action: URGENT: Mow/clear edges to reduce turf and brome grass competition. Hand-weed around seedlings. Do not let weeds shade out the establishing prairie plants.
description: Northeast native seed establishment plot. Seedlings competing with turf and brome grass infiltrating from edges.
goals: Establish native seed plot successfully. Suppress turf competition. Protect emerging native seedlings through their critical first 1–2 years.
target_natives: Prairie seed mix as planted — document what germinates
notes: Native prairie seedlings may look like weeds in years 1–2 — most energy goes to root development above-ground growth is minimal. Be very cautious before pulling anything. Mowing to 4–6 inches in summer suppresses weeds without harming prairie plants. Do not use herbicide near seedlings.

name: Perennial Plot
description: Small dedicated perennial bed. Managed ornamental and native mix.
goals: Maintain as tended perennial bed. Favor native perennials where possible. Good place to trial species before planting larger areas.
notes: Small enough to maintain entirely by hand. Divide overcrowded perennials in spring or fall. Deadhead through summer to extend bloom; leave seedheads standing in fall for birds.
